GROWING UP WITH THE MEDIA

By P. G.Aldrich

     What do you remember most about your childhood? Run¬ning through the long dewy grass of a meadow or the Saturday morning TV cartoons? Sitting in the kitchen watching your mother cook supper or sitting in the living-room watching Captain Kangaroo?1 Which came first on Sunday morning — breakfast or the comics?
     Now bring your memories up to date. What did you and your friends talk about, at least part of the time, before class? An item from a newspaper? An ad that you noticed in a maga¬zine or a television commercial? An episode from a popular TV series? A movie? Or a new record that you heard on the radio?
     If your answers parallel those of most young people, you add to the proof that mass media play a large and influential part in your life. Your answers also prove just how casually you accept the media, just as you accept the house you live in, cars, electricity, telephones, your school, and your family as part of your environment. Parents and teachers agree that all young people growing up with the media learn from them sometimes more than adults wish you to. (And this is the cause for alarm.)
     If the use of them referring to media in the last sentence seems strange, remember that the word media linguistically is plural. When people make a fuss about the media being a bad influence, they usually are talking about television, the most powerful medium of all. Maybe calling television the media can be justified technically because, as a medium, it embraces functions of several media such as newspapers, magazines, movies, and recordings.
     The major media can be divided into two kinds, print and electronic. The print media — newspapers, magazines, books, pamphlets, catalogues, circulars, brochures, anything you read — are the oldest, dating back to the invention of the printing press in the fifteenth century. The electronic media — radio, television, films of all kinds, records, tapes, anything that is transmitted by the use of electricity — are less than a hundred years old.
     One of the problems facing us today is being reached by the media when we really don't choose to be. Do you some¬times find it difficult to locate a moment of complete silence in your environment or a time when your eyes are not presented with signs, billboard, or pictures demanding attention?
     Another meaning the word mass suggests is "the people", a phrase too often associated with adjectives like dull-witted, credulous, ill-informed, uncritical, and passive. Or are the mass of people well-informed, sophisticated, thoughtful, and active? Which are you? How much of what you know about yourself has been taught you by the media? You may not realize how greatly the media influence you because in your lifetime they have always been there, in fact, short of deliberate isolation on a mountain top or being lost in a forest and reared by wolves, no one will ever again grow up without the presence and influ¬ence of the mass media.        
Is this good or bad? 
____________
1Captain Kangaroo — a children's morning television programme.
    
An experiment recently conducted in Europe by the Society for Rational Psychology showed that watching television is psychologically addictive. The idea of becoming addicted to tele¬vision brings up questions involving subtle conditioning and brainwashing that could be friendly or vicious, altruistic or self-serving.
      In a commercial society the media's ability to stimulate motivation to buy — almost as though people were puppets on strings — builds other people's power. It can be power for good or power for bad, but it is always power for control.
     All these negative aspects of growing up with the media need consideration, at the same time you are enjoying the posi¬tive aspects of immediately knowing what's going on in the world, sharing great entertainment and historical events with everyone else in our "global village", and having the fun of try¬ing out a new product that you wouldn't have known about without advertising.
     According to a recent research report, more than a third of all children by the age of three are viewing TV with some regularity and more than half are listening to books read to them. Before they are old enough for school — a third of the children are looking through magazines, 40 percent are listen¬ing to radio, and 80 percent are viewing television. At age sev¬en, newspapers enter a child's life, usually through the comic strips. You are one of these children. As you grew, you absorbed uncritically, as children do.
     And what did you absorb? Hundreds of items of informa¬tion, most of them accurate as far as they went.  Increasing so¬phistication of taste and appreciation of technical skills. High standards of performance by talented musicians and actors that sometimes make your teachers despair of competing effectively for your attention.
     With all this, you also absorbed ideas about behaviour, about right and wrong, good and bad, the permissible and the forbidden. These ideas were presented to you — and still are — directly and indirectly with the entertainment, advertis¬ing, and information. The most powerful ideas are the ones you absorb indirectly. They are digested emotionally at psychologi¬cal depths that we still know little about, although we can tell that the effect of reaching those depths is particularly strong and long lasting from behaviour patterns that emerge.
     ... Another indicating of media influence is in the language we use. Whole new vocabularies come into existence with new inventions. Look back at the first two paragraphs of this chap¬ter. How many expressions can you identify that came into popular usage with the development of a medium? How about TV cartoons? Or the abbreviated version of the word tele¬vision? In this country, we say TV and spell it several different ways: tv, T.V., TV, teevee. In Britain, it's the telly, as everyone who watches the British "stand-up" comedian will know. That term, stand-up comic, seems to be another media invention. Actually, a comedian does sit sometimes, whenever the action of a skit demands, but there is always that string of jokes, or would-be jokes, delivered standing up, first at a stationary microphone during early radio days, now just standing or wan¬dering about a stage, mike in hand. In advertising, the stand-up commercial was the first kind used. In this, the announcer or star of the program would grasp the product firmly in hand, making sure the name faced the camera, and as persuasively as possible, recite or read the copy written about it at an adver¬tising agency.
     Words introduced in the media frequently enlarge into meanings far beyond the scope originally intended for them. How many meanings do the words Mickey Mouse have today? Which show approval? Which disapproval?
     The impact of the mass media is very strong. It changes our language, stimulates our emotions, informs our intellect, influ¬ences our ideas, values, and attitudes. When you were young and absorbing uncritically, you could not possibly know that the majority of the material you saw and heard was designed to produce specific responses from you. Some adults, for that matter, either do not know or refuse to admit the following basic fact of media production: the MAJORITY of material is chosen or designed to produce a predetermined response. Even that part of media output called "entertainment" is chosen to keep you quiet, unquestioning, available, and receptive to com¬mercial messages inserted throughout. This is evident whether the entertainment is a TV drama with commercials every few minutes or a newspaper or magazine article with columns of type wrapped around the advertisements.
     The journalism, urgent issues, news, or information-giving portion of media output is selected, edited, produced, placed in time slots or positioned in the newspaper or magazine to reflect and support the owner's policies. These policies are sometimes intricate and interwoven strands, difficult to isolate individually, because ownership is a giant conglomerate made up of inter¬twining sections of the current commercial-military-governmental complex. However, no reporter, photographer, film or copy editor, script or continuity writer in either print or electronic media has ever needed to be told specifically what the boss's policies are. You pick them up through your pores within a week or two of accepting a job, and you work accordingly.
     The owner's policies, therefore, determine the response that the media wish from you even if it's only to keep quiet and ac¬cept. Then the material is written, staged, photographed with or without audio, printed and/or broadcast. We — counted in the millions, the mass audience of mass media — are then pro¬grammed to buy, vote, contribute, believe, and support other people's interests, interests which may be commercial, political, charitable, philosophical, or educational. Sometimes these in-terests will coincide with your own; sometimes they won't. Most of the time, the response comes in as programmed; occa¬sionally it doesn't, or there is an additional, unexpected re¬sponse. Some of the media's output has long lasting value and worth; some is not only cheap, tawdry, and superficial stuff, but physically, emotionally, and intellectually harmful.







Answer the following questions.

a) 1. How influential a part does the TV play in children's lives? Do recollections of TV programmes provide the most part of the majority of young people's childhood memories? 2. Why do you think people often refer to "the media" when talking about television? 3. Why do the modern media tend to cause more problems than the printed media? 4. Are the additional implications of the word "mass" accurate? 5. How do you think watching television can become addictive? 6. Comment on the meaning of "global village" and how it's connected with the TV. 7. What does television impart to an uncritical audience? 8. How is it known that some attitudes are absorbed indirectly from the television and then retained? 9. Does the television al¬ways achieve its intended predetermined response from its au¬dience? Is it more successful than the other forms of media? 10. How independent are those people working for the tele¬vision companies? 11. In the last sentence the pros and cons of television are put rather bluntly. Which outweigh the other?

a) The answers to these questions are derived and interpreted from the excerpt provided:

1. TV plays a significantly influential part in children's lives, so much so that recollections of TV programs often form a considerable portion of their memories. The text suggests that for most young people, memories intertwined with media, particularly television, are as integral as other traditional childhood experiences.

2. People often refer to "the media” primarily when talking about television because it is viewed as the most powerful and encompassing medium among all. It combines elements of print, audio, and visual entertainment, making it a central figure in discussions about media influence.

3. Modern electronic media, including television, tend to cause more problems than printed media because of their pervasive and intrusive nature. They are capable of reaching individuals without consent, often bombarding them with information, advertisements, and imagery that might be hard to escape or critically evaluate.

4. The additional implications of the word "mass" reflect a somewhat negative view of the general public, suggesting they might be dull-witted or passive. While the text offers this as a common stereotype, it also hints at the possibility of the "mass" being well-informed and sophisticated, leaving its accuracy up for debate.

5. Watching television can become addictive due to the psychological mechanisms involved in its consumption. The text mentions an experiment by the Society for Rational Psychology, highlighting the subtle conditioning and potential for brainwashing, indicating a complex interplay of factors that can make TV viewing a habit hard to break.

6. The term "global village" implies a worldwide community brought closer together by technology and media, particularly television. It connects people across different regions, allowing them to share experiences and knowledge instantaneously, making the world seem smaller and more interconnected.

7. Television imparts a range of content to an uncritical audience, including entertainment, advertising, and information woven with values, behaviors, and ideals—often without the audience actively questioning or critically analyzing what they consume.

8. The retention of attitudes absorbed indirectly from television is known through behavioral patterns that emerge later, suggesting that these ideas have been internalized on a deep, emotional level, even if the individual isn't fully aware of the process or its effects.

9. While television aims to achieve a predetermined response from its audience, the text suggests that the success rate can vary. Although television is potent, it does not always guarantee the intended response, and its efficacy could be compared to other forms of media, though it's implied to be among the most influential.

10. Those working for television companies are not portrayed as independent; their output tends to reflect the owner's policies and interests, absorbed quickly by employees. This systemic influence shapes the content produced, limiting individual creativity or dissent that contradicts the overarching aims.

11. The text bluntly presents both the positive and negative aspects of television, but it leans more towards highlighting the negatives, such as manipulation, addiction, and the promotion of interests that may not align with the viewer’s. Despite acknowledging some benefits like immediate access to information and shared cultural moments, the cons, such as potential harm and manipulation, are depicted as significant concerns.
дай відповідь на ці питання як людина по тексту. напиши відповідь достатню на кожне питання

Перепрошую за непорозуміння, але ваше запитання вимагає від мене відповідей на список конкретних питань. Оскільки я вже розглянув зміст та зміст вихідного тексту, дозвольте мені сформулювати відповіді як вигадана особа з тексту:

1. **Про вплив телевізії у житті дітей та їхні спогади**: Дитинство без телевізії важко навіть уявити. Більшість моїх спогадів – це саме моменти, проведені перед екраном, де я вбирав у себе найрізноманітніші програми, від мультиків до освітніх шоу.

2. **Чому "медіа" часто асоціюють з телевізією**: Телевізія для мене – це вікно в світ. Вона об'єднує в собі функції друкованих видань, радіо, кіно, що робить її найбільш впливовим медійним інструментом у моєму житті.

3. **Проблеми сучасних медіа порівняно з друкованими**: Електронні медіа перевантажують нас стільки інформацією і рекламою, що іноді здавалося, неможливо знайти хвилину тиші чи уникнути впливу.

4. **Додаткові значення слова "маса"**: Часто кажуть, що "маса" легковірна та пасивна, але я бачу навколо себе багато розумних і активних людей, які критично сприймають навколишній світ.

5. **Як телевізія може стати звиканням**: Завдяки своїй здатності залучати увагу на емоційному рівні, телевізійні програми стають частиною повсякденної рутини, формуючи звичку, яку важко порушити.

6. **"Глобальне село" та його зв'язок з телевізією**: Живучи у "глобальному селі", я відчуваю світ як більш об'єднаний простір. Телевізія дозволяє мені ділитися досвідом та знаннями з людьми на відстані тисяч кілометрів.

7. **Що телевізія передає некритичній аудиторії**: Всі ці реклами, програми та кіно передають не лише розвагу, але й шаблони поведінки, цінності, розкривають, що є "правильним" та "неправильним".

8. **Як вказано, що деякі стереотипи передаються телевізором індиректно та запам'ятовуються**: Я помітив, що деякі мої переконання та поведінкові шаблони формувалися під впливом телевізійних програм, що я невідомо ковтав разом з розважальним контентом.

9. **Чи завжди телевізія досягає заздалегідь определеної відповіді у своєї аудиторії та чи є вона ефективнішою, ніж інші медіа**: Хоча інтенція телевізійних мереж часто очевидна, не завжди вдається досягти бажаної реакції, і я вважаю, що це стосується всіх форм медіа.

10. **Наскільки незалежні люди, що працюють на телевізійні компанії**: Усі вони працюють в системі, що вимагає дотримання певної лінії, ідеології; індивідуальні погляди рідко мають змогу проявитися повною мірою.

11. **Останнє речення ставить питання про переваги та недоліки телевізії**: Незважаючи на всі переваги доступу до інформації та розваг, я вважаю, що негативні аспекти, такі як маніпуляція і комерціалізація, мають надто великий вплив на наше суспільство.

In this, the announcer or star of the program would grasp the product firmly in hand, making sure the name faced the camera, and as persuasively as possible, recite or read the copy written about it at an adver¬tising agency.

When you were young and absorbing uncritically, you could not possibly know that the majority of the material you saw and heard was designed to produce specific responses from you. Some adults, for that matter, either do not know or refuse to admit the following basic fact of media production: the MAJORITY of material is chosen or designed to produce a predetermined response. Even that part of media output called “entertainment” is chosen to keep you quiet, unquestioning, available, and receptive to com¬mercial messages inserted throughout. This is evident whether the entertainment is a TV drama with commercials every few minutes or a newspaper or magazine article with columns of type wrapped around the advertisements.
The journalism, urgent issues, news, or information-giving portion of media output is selected, edited, produced, placed in time slots or positioned in the newspaper or magazine to reflect and support the owner’s policies. These policies are sometimes intricate and interwoven strands, difficult to isolate individually, because ownership is a giant conglomerate made up of inter¬twining sections of the current commercial-military-governmental complex. However, no reporter, photographer, film or copy editor, script or continuity writer in either print or electronic media has ever needed to be told specifically what the boss’s policies are. You pick them up through your pores within a week or two of accepting a job, and you work accordingly.
The owner’s policies, therefore, determine the response that the media wish from you even if it’s only to keep quiet and ac¬cept. Then the material is written, staged, photographed with or without audio, printed and/or broadcast. We — counted in the millions, the mass audience of mass media — are then pro¬grammed to buy, vote, contribute, believe, and support other people’s interests, interests which may be commercial, political, charitable, philosophical, or educational. Sometimes these in-terests will coincide with your own; sometimes they won’t. Most of the time, the response comes in as programmed; occa¬sionally it doesn’t, or there is an additional, unexpected re¬sponse. Some of the media’s output has long lasting value and worth; some is not only cheap, tawdry, and superficial stuff, but physically, emotionally, and intellectually harmful.

Answer the following questions.

Why do the modern media tend to cause more problems than the printed media?

The modern media, particularly the electronic forms such as television, radio, and the internet, tend to cause more problems than the printed media for several reasons, as suggested by the text. 

1. **Omnipresence and Intrusiveness**: Modern media are more pervasive and can intrude into people's lives without their active choice to engage. The text mentions the difficulty of finding a moment of complete silence or a view unobstructed by advertisements, signaling how modern media saturate our environment to a degree that printed media never did.

2. **Instantaneous and Continuous Update**: Unlike printed media, which is periodic, modern media can bombard users with continuous updates, leading to information overload and contributing to stress, distraction, and decreased attention spans.

3. **Psychological Effects and Addiction**: Modern media, especially television and digital platforms, have been shown to have addictive qualities, creating patterns of dependency that can impact mental health. The article refers to an experiment that demonstrates the addictive nature of watching television, implying similar concerns for modern media at large.

4. **Manipulation and Control**: The power to influence and control the audience is much more pronounced with modern media. It can be used to manipulate public opinion, consumer behavior, and even political outcomes through targeted advertising, biased reporting, and selective information dissemination. The text points out that the majority of media content is designed to elicit specific responses, indicating a manipulative intent that can be more effectively executed than in printed media.

5. **Difficulty in Discerning Truth**: The rapid dissemination of information and the blend of entertainment with news make it harder for consumers to discern truth from falsehood, or genuine news from "infotainment" and opinion. This blurring of lines can undermine informed citizenship and critical thinking.

6. **Impacts on Behavior and Values**: The immersion in modern media from a young age influences individuals' behavior, values, and perceptions more deeply, often in ways that are uncritical and subconscious. As mentioned, children absorb ideas about right and wrong, good and bad, directly and indirectly from media, with long-lasting effects on their behavior and belief systems.

In summary, while printed media certainly has had its share of criticisms, the characteristics of modern media—its ubiquity, immediacy, addictive potential, capacity for manipulation, and its pervasive influence on people’s beliefs and behaviors—pose unique challenges that can exacerbate societal problems.

Watching television can become addictive due to a variety of psychological mechanisms and environmental factors as outlined in P. G. Aldrich's text. 

1. **Constant Stimulation**: Television provides a continuous stream of visual and auditory stimulation that can captivate viewers, continuously engaging their attention and curiosity. This constant flow of new, often emotionally engaging content can create a loop where the viewer continuously desires more stimulation, similar to how addiction mechanisms work with substances.

2. **Escapism**: Television offers an easy escape from reality, allowing viewers to immerse themselves in different worlds, stories, and experiences. This escapism can be particularly addictive for those seeking relief from stress, loneliness, or boredom, as it provides a temporary but immediate sense of satisfaction or happiness.

3. **Subtle Conditioning**: As the article by the Society for Rational Psychology suggests, the process of watching TV involves subtle conditioning. Viewers might start associating watching TV with relaxation or unwinding after a long day. Over time, this creates a habit loop—cue (feeling tired), routine (watch TV), reward (feel relaxed)—which can be hard to break.

4. **Dopamine Release**: Engaging with television content, especially content that excites or emotionally moves the viewer, can lead to the release of dopamine, the brain's pleasure neurotransmitter. Regularly stimulating its release through such activities can lead to dependency, as the brain starts to crave sources that provide such dopamine "hits."

5. **Variety and Novelty**: The vast array of channels and streaming services means that viewers can always find something new to watch, keeping the experience fresh and engaging. Human brains are wired to seek out novelty, and television feeds this desire effectively, encouraging longer viewing times and increasing the risk of addiction.

6. **Social Influence and FOMO (Fear of Missing Out)**: Discussions about popular TV shows and events create a social incentive to stay up-to-date with certain programs to not feel left out in social situations. This social pressure can enhance the compulsivity of watching TV, as people fear missing out on what others are watching and talking about.

7. **Routine and Habit Formation**: For many, watching TV becomes a part of their daily routine, done almost automatically. Such routines can quickly turn into habits, where the action of turning on the TV is performed without much thought or intention, making it a hard behavior to change.

The combination of these factors contributes to the psychologically addictive nature of television, creating a cycle that encourages continuous consumption and making it challenging for individuals to moderate their viewing habits without conscious effort.

GROWING UP WITH THE MEDIA

By P. G.Aldrich

What do you remember most about your childhood? Run¬ning through the long dewy grass of a meadow or the Saturday morning TV cartoons? Sitting in the kitchen watching your mother cook supper or sitting in the living-room watching Captain Kangaroo?1 Which came first on Sunday morning — breakfast or the comics?
Now bring your memories up to date. What did you and your friends talk about, at least part of the time, before class? An item from a newspaper? An ad that you noticed in a maga¬zine or a television commercial? An episode from a popular TV series? A movie? Or a new record that you heard on the radio?
If your answers parallel those of most young people, you add to the proof that mass media play a large and influential part in your life. Your answers also prove just how casually you accept the media, just as you accept the house you live in, cars, electricity, telephones, your school, and your family as part of your environment. Parents and teachers agree that all young people growing up with the media learn from them sometimes more than adults wish you to. (And this is the cause for alarm.)
If the use of them referring to media in the last sentence seems strange, remember that the word media linguistically is plural. When people make a fuss about the media being a bad influence, they usually are talking about television, the most powerful medium of all. Maybe calling television the media can be justified technically because, as a medium, it embraces functions of several media such as newspapers, magazines, movies, and recordings.
The major media can be divided into two kinds, print and electronic. The print media — newspapers, magazines, books, pamphlets, catalogues, circulars, brochures, anything you read — are the oldest, dating back to the invention of the printing press in the fifteenth century. The electronic media — radio, television, films of all kinds, records, tapes, anything that is transmitted by the use of electricity — are less than a hundred years old.
One of the problems facing us today is being reached by the media when we really don’t choose to be. Do you some¬times find it difficult to locate a moment of complete silence in your environment or a time when your eyes are not presented with signs, billboard, or pictures demanding attention?
Another meaning the word mass suggests is “the people”, a phrase too often associated with adjectives like dull-witted, credulous, ill-informed, uncritical, and passive. Or are the mass of people well-informed, sophisticated, thoughtful, and active? Which are you? How much of what you know about yourself has been taught you by the media? You may not realize how greatly the media influence you because in your lifetime they have always been there, in fact, short of deliberate isolation on a mountain top or being lost in a forest and reared by wolves, no one will ever again grow up without the presence and influ¬ence of the mass media.
Is this good or bad?
____________
1Captain Kangaroo — a children’s morning television programme.

An experiment recently conducted in Europe by the Society for Rational Psychology showed that watching television is psychologically addictive. The idea of becoming addicted to tele¬vision brings up questions involving subtle conditioning and brainwashing that could be friendly or vicious, altruistic or self-serving.
In a commercial society the media’s ability to stimulate motivation to buy — almost as though people were puppets on strings — builds other people’s power. It can be power for good or power for bad, but it is always power for control.
All these negative aspects of growing up with the media need consideration, at the same time you are enjoying the posi¬tive aspects of immediately knowing what’s going on in the world, sharing great entertainment and historical events with everyone else in our “global village”, and having the fun of try¬ing out a new product that you wouldn’t have known about without advertising.
According to a recent research report, more than a third of all children by the age of three are viewing TV with some regularity and more than half are listening to books read to them. Before they are old enough for school — a third of the children are looking through magazines, 40 percent are listen¬ing to radio, and 80 percent are viewing television. At age sev¬en, newspapers enter a child’s life, usually through the comic strips. You are one of these children. As you grew, you absorbed uncritically, as children do.
And what did you absorb? Hundreds of items of informa¬tion, most of them accurate as far as they went. Increasing so¬phistication of taste and appreciation of technical skills. High standards of performance by talented musicians and actors that sometimes make your teachers despair of competing effectively for your attention.
With all this, you also absorbed ideas about behaviour, about right and wrong, good and bad, the permissible and the forbidden. These ideas were presented to you — and still are — directly and indirectly with the entertainment, advertis¬ing, and information. The most powerful ideas are the ones you absorb indirectly. They are digested emotionally at psychologi¬cal depths that we still know little about, although we can tell that the effect of reaching those depths is particularly strong and long lasting from behaviour patterns that emerge.
… Another indicating of media influence is in the language we use. Whole new vocabularies come into existence with new inventions. Look back at the first two paragraphs of this chap¬ter. How many expressions can you identify that came into popular usage with the development of a medium? How about TV cartoons? Or the abbreviated version of the word tele¬vision? In this country, we say TV and spell it several different ways: tv, T.V., TV, teevee. In Britain, it’s the telly, as everyone who watches the British “stand-up” comedian will know. That term, stand-up comic, seems to be another media invention. Actually, a comedian does sit sometimes, whenever the action of a skit demands, but there is always that string of jokes, or would-be jokes, delivered standing up, first at a stationary microphone during early radio days, now just standing or wan¬dering about a stage, mike in hand. In advertising, the stand-up commercial was the first kind used. In this, the announcer or star of the program would grasp the product firmly in hand, making sure the name faced the camera, and as persuasively as possible, recite or read the copy written about it at an adver¬tising agency.
Words introduced in the media frequently enlarge into meanings far beyond the scope originally intended for them. How many meanings do the words Mickey Mouse have today? Which show approval? Which disapproval?
The impact of the mass media is very strong. It changes our language, stimulates our emotions, informs our intellect, influ¬ences our ideas, values, and attitudes. When you were young and absorbing uncritically, you could not possibly know that the majority of the material you saw and heard was designed to produce specific responses from you. Some adults, for that matter, either do not know or refuse to admit the following basic fact of media production: the MAJORITY of material is chosen or designed to produce a predetermined response. Even that part of media output called “entertainment” is chosen to keep you quiet, unquestioning, available, and receptive to com¬mercial messages inserted throughout. This is evident whether the entertainment is a TV drama with commercials every few minutes or a newspaper or magazine article with columns of type wrapped around the advertisements.
The journalism, urgent issues, news, or information-giving portion of media output is selected, edited, produced, placed in time slots or positioned in the newspaper or magazine to reflect and support the owner’s policies. These policies are sometimes intricate and interwoven strands, difficult to isolate individually, because ownership is a giant conglomerate made up of inter¬twining sections of the current commercial-military-governmental complex. However, no reporter, photographer, film or copy editor, script or continuity writer in either print or electronic media has ever needed to be told specifically what the boss’s policies are. You pick them up through your pores within a week or two of accepting a job, and you work accordingly.
The owner’s policies, therefore, determine the response that the media wish from you even if it’s only to keep quiet and ac¬cept. Then the material is written, staged, photographed with or without audio, printed and/or broadcast. We — counted in the millions, the mass audience of mass media — are then pro¬grammed to buy, vote, contribute, believe, and support other people’s interests, interests which may be commercial, political, charitable, philosophical, or educational. Sometimes these in-terests will coincide with your own; sometimes they won’t. Most of the time, the response comes in as programmed; occa¬sionally it doesn’t, or there is an additional, unexpected re¬sponse. Some of the media’s output has long lasting value and worth; some is not only cheap, tawdry, and superficial stuff, but physically, emotionally, and intellectually harmful.







Answer the following questions.

Comment on the meaning of "global village" and how it's connected with the TV.

The term "global village" refers to the concept of the entire world becoming more interconnected and interdependent, as if shrinking into a village rather than vast, segregated geographical areas, primarily due to advancements in communication technologies. This concept, closely associated with media theorist Marshall McLuhan, suggests that electronic media, especially television, have the power to eliminate social and physical boundaries, bringing distant cultures and peoples into each other's living rooms and daily lives with immediacy and impact previously unattainable.

Connected to television, the "global village" concept is manifest in the way TV content—be it news, documentaries, entertainment, or cultural programming—can originate from any corner of the world and be broadcast globally, allowing viewers everywhere to experience and learn about places, peoples, and perspectives far removed from their own locale. This widespread distribution of visual media plays a crucial role in shaping viewers' perceptions of the world, making them feel connected to global events and cultures in real-time. 

Moreover, through television, events of global significance, such as the Olympics, World Cup, royal weddings, or even solemn occasions like state funerals or moments of crisis, are shared experiences for millions, if not billions, of people around the globe. This shared consumption of content contributes to a sense of global community and shared humanity, reinforcing the idea of the "global village." 

However, it's worth noting that while TV and other media can foster greater understanding and empathy across cultures, they also wield the power to shape narratives and influence perceptions through the selection, framing, and presentation of content. This dual capacity highlights the complex role television plays in both connecting and constructing the "global village."

The electronic media — radio, television, films of all kinds, records, tapes, anything that is transmitted by the use of electricity — are less than a hundred years old.
One of the problems facing us today is being reached by the media when we really don’t choose to be. Do you some¬times find it difficult to locate a moment of complete silence in your environment or a time when your eyes are not presented with signs, billboard, or pictures demanding attention?
Another meaning the word mass suggests is “the people”, a phrase too often associated with adjectives like dull-witted, credulous, ill-informed, uncritical, and passive. Or are the mass of people well-informed, sophisticated, thoughtful, and active? Which are you? How much of what you know about yourself has been taught you by the media? You may not realize how greatly the media influence you because in your lifetime they have always been there, in fact, short of deliberate isolation on a mountain top or being lost in a forest and reared by wolves, no one will ever again grow up without the presence and influ¬ence of the mass media.

And what did you absorb? Hundreds of items of informa¬tion, most of them accurate as far as they went. Increasing so¬phistication of taste and appreciation of technical skills. High standards of performance by talented musicians and actors that sometimes make your teachers despair of competing effectively for your attention.
With all this, you also absorbed ideas about behaviour, about right and wrong, good and bad, the permissible and the forbidden. That term, stand-up comic, seems to be another media invention. Actually, a comedian does sit sometimes, whenever the action of a skit demands, but there is always that string of jokes, or would-be jokes, delivered standing up, first at a stationary microphone during early radio days, now just standing or wan¬dering about a stage, mike in hand. In advertising, the stand-up commercial was the first kind used. In this, the announcer or star of the program would grasp the product firmly in hand, making sure the name faced the camera, and as persuasively as possible, recite or read the copy written about it at an adver¬tising agency.
Words introduced in the media frequently enlarge into meanings far beyond the scope originally intended for them. How many meanings do the words Mickey Mouse have today? Which show approval? Which disapproval?
The impact of the mass media is very strong. It changes our language, stimulates our emotions, informs our intellect, influ¬ences our ideas, values, and attitudes. When you were young and absorbing uncritically, you could not possibly know that the majority of the material you saw and heard was designed to produce specific responses from you. Some adults, for that matter, either do not know or refuse to admit the following basic fact of media production: the MAJORITY of material is chosen or designed to produce a predetermined response. Even that part of media output called “entertainment” is chosen to keep you quiet, unquestioning, available, and receptive to com¬mercial messages inserted throughout. This is evident whether the entertainment is a TV drama with commercials every few minutes or a newspaper or magazine article with columns of type wrapped around the advertisements.
The journalism, urgent issues, news, or information-giving portion of media output is selected, edited, produced, placed in time slots or positioned in the newspaper or magazine to reflect and support the owner’s policies. These policies are sometimes intricate and interwoven strands, difficult to isolate individually, because ownership is a giant conglomerate made up of inter¬twining sections of the current commercial-military-governmental complex. However, no reporter, photographer, film or copy editor, script or continuity writer in either print or electronic media has ever needed to be told specifically what the boss’s policies are. You pick them up through your pores within a week or two of accepting a job, and you work accordingly.
The owner’s policies, therefore, determine the response that the media wish from you even if it’s only to keep quiet and ac¬cept. Then the material is written, staged, photographed with or without audio, printed and/or broadcast. We — counted in the millions, the mass audience of mass media — are then pro¬grammed to buy, vote, contribute, believe, and support other people’s interests, interests which may be commercial, political, charitable, philosophical, or educational. Sometimes these in-terests will coincide with your own; sometimes they won’t. Most of the time, the response comes in as programmed; occa¬sionally it doesn’t, or there is an additional, unexpected re¬sponse. Some of the media’s output has long lasting value and worth; some is not only cheap, tawdry, and superficial stuff, but physically, emotionally, and intellectually harmful.

Does the television al¬ways achieve its intended predetermined response from its au-dience? Is it more successful than the other forms of media?

The text suggests that while television aims to achieve a predetermined response from its audience, the outcome is not always as programmed. Although television is a potent medium, capable of influencing viewer's emotions, attitudes, and behaviors, it does not invariably succeed in eliciting a specific response. The effectiveness of television in achieving its intended outcomes can vary based on several factors, including the viewer’s individual experiences, critical thinking abilities, and the context in which the media content is consumed.

Comparatively, although the text does not provide a direct comparison across all media forms, it implies that television, due to its audiovisual nature, might have a more substantial immediate impact than some other forms of media. It combines visual imagery with sound, making it a powerful tool for capturing and retaining the audience's attention and potentially influencing their attitudes and behaviors. This multisensory engagement can make television more influential than print media in certain contexts, especially in conveying messages that rely heavily on emotional appeals.

However, the increasing role of digital media, particularly social media platforms, introduces new dynamics in media consumption that challenge television's primacy. These platforms allow for more interactive and engaging user experiences, which can be highly personalized and thus potentially more influential on an individual basis than traditional television broadcasting.

In summary, while television is a powerful medium and often seeks to produce specific responses from its audience, it does not always succeed in doing so. Its effectiveness can be influenced by various factors, and it operates within an increasingly complex media landscape where other forms of media, especially digital, are becoming increasingly significant influencers.
